JS From Zero (Day 18): localStorage — Save Data Right in the Browser
Day eighteen of the reincarnation. This time I want to show you another way to store data — localStorage.

localStorage is one of the newer things that came in with HTML5. There are actually two flavors of this kind of storage: sessionStorage, and the one we're covering here, localStorage. The main difference between the two is just the lifecycle.

Normally, when a web page needs to stash some data temporarily, one option is to store it in a Cookie. The thing is, a Cookie can only hold about 4 KB — go even a little over that and it blows up. So Cookies are usually used for login info and similar small stuff. If the data you want to store is bigger than 4 KB, you can switch to localStorage instead.
The other nice thing about localStorage is that the data sticks around basically forever. Unless the user deletes it, whatever you put in localStorage stays in the browser.
